content.problemStatement:
Consider a string sss. The following operation can be performed on it successively several (possibly zero) times.


Select any non empty suffix of sss and append it to sss
For example, if s=abcds = \text{abcd}s=abcd, then sss can become either abcdd\text{abcdd}abcdd, abcdcd\text{abcdcd}abcdcd, abcdbcd\text{abcdbcd}abcdbcd or abcdabcd\text{abcdabcd}abcdabcd after one operation. Successive operations are now performed on the new string.

You are given a string ttt, the resultant string after applying several (possibly zero) operations on some string. You must find any such string sss of the **shortest** length from which ttt can be obtained after some sequence of operations. It can be proven that such a string always exists.


Input Format


The first line contains one number TTT, the number of test cases.




The only line of each test case contains the string ttt.





Output Format
For each test case print a string sss of the shortest possible length which satisfies the requirements. If there are multiple such strings, you may print any.
constraints: Sum of |t| over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a non‑empty string t consisting of lowercase English letters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a string s on its own line – the shortest possible string from which t can be obtained using the described operations. If several strings of minimal length exist, output any of them.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each string the answer is the prefix that contains the first occurrence of every distinct character. In the first two cases the distinct characters are 'a' and 'b', so the prefix "ab" suffices. In the third case the distinct characters are c,o,d,e,h,f and their last first occurrence is at position 7, giving the prefix "codechef".
In "a" there is only one distinct character, so the whole string is needed. In "abcabc" the distinct characters are a,b,c and their first appearances end at index 2, thus the minimal prefix is "abc".

solution.approach: Observe that a character that appears for the first time in t cannot be produced by copying a suffix, because every suffix consists only of characters already present. Therefore the initial string s must contain every character at its first occurrence. Let p be the position of the last first occurrence of any character in t. The prefix t[0..p] (inclusive) satisfies the requirement: after p all characters are repeats, so each of them can be generated by copying a suffix of length 1 (the character itself). Consequently the minimal possible length of s is exactly p+1, and any prefix of that length works. The algorithm scans t from left to right, keeps a boolean array of size 26 to mark seen characters, and records the index when the count of seen characters becomes equal to the total number of distinct characters in t. The answer is the substring up to that index.
solution.timeComplexity: O(|t|) per test case, total O(Σ|t|).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a 26‑element boolean array and a few integers.

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    if not data: return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        t = next(it)
        distinct = len(set(t))
        seen = set()
        pos = 0
        for i,ch in enumerate(t):
            seen.add(ch)
            if len(seen) == distinct:
                pos = i
                break
        out.append(t[:pos+1])
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
